query: does erenumab bind to calcitonin gene-related peptide

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Drug interactions and comorbidities also affect the dosage of gepants, so they should also undergo periodic assessment; this requires proper coordination between professionals in different specialties. In the case of mAbs such as erenumab, patient education by health providers is necessary for patient compliance. These medications need a prolonged time before any significant relief can be appreciated. The pharmacist will also need to perform medication reconciliation to address drug interactions and verify all dosing is appropriate.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Erenumab is a human immunoglobulin G2 (IgG2) monoclonal antibody produced from Chinese hamster ovary cells. Erenumab acts by potently and competitively inhibiting the binding of CGRP to its receptor. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, eptinezumab, galcanezumab, fremanezumab) are used for migraine prophylaxis in episodic and chronic migraine. Rimegepant and ubrogepant are used for acute treatment of migraine with or without aura. galcanezumab and fremanezumab are under development for the preventive treatment of cluster headaches.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, there are no guidelines in place for monitoring CGRP receptor-acting drugs. However, the general monitoring of responsiveness to monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, eptinezumab, galcanezumab, and fremanezumab) against the CGRP receptor is by periodic assessment of changes in mean Monthly Migraine Days (MMD) or monthly Migraine Physical Function Impact Diary (MPFID) activity scores. Changes in these values usually occur 3 to 4 months after starting the drug.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Erenumab is a self-administrable subcutaneous injection. The recommended dosage is 70 mg subcutaneously once or twice a month in the abdomen, thigh, or upper arm. Alternatively, the dosage of 140 mg may be administered once a month. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Erenumab : The only contraindication for the use of erenumab is hypersensitivity to drugs or excipients. The current commercially available form of erenumab makes use of substances such as latex. Allergic history to such substances should also be obtained before administration. [13] Eptinezumab, galcanezumab, fremanezumab: These drugs are contraindicated in patients with serious hypersensitivity to these or any of their excipients. [9] [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, FDA-approved drugs acting on the CGRP, or its receptor, are those used in the treatment or prophylaxis of migraine. This group includes monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, eptinezumab, galcanezumab, fremanezumab) against the CGRP receptor and CGRP receptor antagonists (rimegepant and ubrogepant). In addition, atogepant and zavegepant are other receptor antagonists that have received FDA approval.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: The first four drugs listed are monoclonal antibodies used in the prophylaxis of chronic and episodic migraine.

retrieved_docs.title: Migraine Medications -- Function -- Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide (CGRP)
retrieved_docs.content: CGRP monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) are the only class of currently used preventives explicitly developed for the treatment of migraines. The current thinking is that CGRP mediates the vasodilatory component of neurogenic inflammation, as CGRP is a widely distributed vasodilator. The CGRP mAbs target either the CGRP molecule itself or the CGRP receptor. In network meta-analysis, the CGRP mAbs seemed to be as effective as other preventive treatments, but have fewer side effects. [14] Long-term data on safety, however, is limited.  These medications include erenumab, fremanezumab, and galcanezumab.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: There is limited clinical experience with drugs acting on CGRP or its receptor. However, treatment should include general supportive measures, including monitoring of the vital signs and observation of the general clinical status. There is no specific antidote for the toxicity of erenumab, eptinezumab, galcanezumab, fremanezumab, rimegepant or, ubrogepant. [13] [9] [10] [11] [15] [16] For rimegepant, dialysis is unlikely to remove rimegepant significantly due to its high serum protein binding. [15] Since the half-life of ubrogepant elimination is about 5 to 7 hours, patient monitoring after ubrogepant overdose should continue while symptoms or signs persist or for a minimum of 24 hours. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Erenumab : The adverse effects reported during the clinical studies of erenumab were injection site reactions such as injection site pain, erythema, and pruritus. There were also reports of constipation, cramps, and muscle spasms. Renal or hepatic impairment is not expected to affect the pharmacokinetics of erenumab. However, sufficient studies have not been conducted to establish the same. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: These monoclonal antibodies act rapidly and prevent the binding of CGRP. This receptor blockade is long-term, which leads to a reliable method of migraine prophylaxis using only a few doses of the drug per month. These drugs are degraded into small peptides and amino acids by enzymatic proteolysis before excretion, as with other monoclonal antibodies.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Fremanezumab is administered as a subcutaneous injection. The recommended dose is 225 mg monthly or 675 mg every three months, administered as three consecutive injections of 225 mg each. Subcutaneous injection is administered in the abdomen, thigh, back of the upper arm, or buttocks. This drug provides effective prophylaxis against episodic and chronic migraines by reducing the mean number of migraine attacks per month and improvement in the mean monthly Migraine Physical Function Impact Diary (MPFID). [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Calcitonin gene-related peptide receptors (CGRP) receptor antagonists and monoclonal antibodies against CGRP and its receptor are medications used in the management and treatment of migraine, which is the sixth-highest cause worldwide of years lost due to disability as per the Global Burden of Disease Study (2013). This activity describes the indications, actions, and contraindications for these drugs as valuable agents in the prophylaxis and acute management of migraine and cluster headaches. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, monitoring, adverse events, relevant interactions, and other key elements in the clinical setting as relates to the essential points needed by members of an interprofessional team managing the care of patients with migraines.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Galcanezumab is administered as a subcutaneous injection. A loading dosage of 240 mg (administered as two consecutive injections of 120 mg each), followed by monthly doses of 120 mg, is recommended. Subcutaneous injection is administered in the abdomen, thigh, back of the upper arm, or buttocks. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Fremanezumab is a fully humanized IgG2 produced by recombinant DNA technology in Chinese hamster ovary (CHO) cells. Fremanezumab selectively targets and binds to both the α and β isoforms of CGRP and prevents CGRP-induced receptor activation. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 335
retrieved_docs.content: Substance with peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action represents a broad class of pharmacologic agents that block the activity of peptide hormone receptors. Within this overarching category, Substance with receptor antagonist mechanism of action (substance) is identified as a more specific subclass of agents that exert their therapeutic effect by antagonizing receptors in general. A particular example of this subclass is antagonisté CGRP receptorů, which comprises compounds that specifically inhibit the calcitonin gene‑related peptide (CGRP) receptor. Thus, CGRP receptor antagonists are situated under the umbrella of receptor‑antagonist substances, which in turn are encompassed by the larger group of peptide‑hormone receptor antagonists. This hierarchical organization clarifies how these drug classes relate to one another and aids clinicians in understanding the scope of receptor‑targeted therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2450
retrieved_docs.content: Calcitonin is biologically related to parathormone, the principal parathyroid hormone. Parathormone is classified within the broader group of parathyroid hormones and analogues, a therapeutic class that also encompasses the synthetic analogue teriparatide. Understanding the connections among calcitonin, parathormone, and their related analogues aids clinicians in comprehending calcium‑regulating pathways and in selecting appropriate hormone‑based treatments for disorders of bone metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870
retrieved_docs.content: Icatibant is classified among the drugs used to treat hereditary angioedema, a rare genetic disorder characterized by episodic swelling of the skin and mucosal tissues. As a bradykinin B2‑receptor antagonist, icatibant specifically blocks the action of bradykinin, a key mediator of the swelling attacks in hereditary angioedema, thereby providing rapid relief of symptoms during acute episodes. Inclusion of icatibant in the therapeutic armamentarium underscores its role as a targeted pharmacologic option for managing this condition and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified as a member of the “parathyroid hormones and analogues” group. It is employed therapeutically to correct نقص كالسيوم الدم (blood calcium deficiency or hypocalcemia), reflecting its central role in raising serum calcium levels. Calcitonin is described as having a relationship with parathormon, highlighting the complementary and often opposing actions of these two hormones in the regulation of calcium metabolism. Understanding the classification of parathormon, its use in treating hypocalcemia, and its interaction with calcitonin is essential for managing disorders of calcium balance and for guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1747
retrieved_docs.content: Atrakurium, imatinib mesylate, teriparatide, and etodolac are each contraindicated in patients who have drug hypersensitivity (فرط الحساسية الدوائية), meaning that these agents should be avoided in individuals with a known allergic reaction to medications. In addition, teriparatide is classified as a member of the “parathyroid hormones and analogues” group, linking it to other agents that act on the parathyroid hormone pathway. Recognizing the contraindication of these drugs in the context of drug hypersensitivity and understanding teriparatide’s membership in the parathyroid hormone class are important for safe prescribing and for selecting appropriate therapeutic options in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 736
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified within the functional category “hormones by function” and belongs to the group “parathyroid hormones and analogues.” It is therapeutically employed to treat نقص كالسيوم الدم (blood calcium deficiency or hypocalcemia). Calcitonin has a physiological relationship with parathormon, with the two hormones exerting opposing actions that regulate calcium balance in the body. Teriparatid is an analogue that is a member of the “parathyroid hormones and analogues” class, mimicking the activity of parathormon. Together, these connections underscore the central role of parathormon and its analogues in maintaining calcium homeostasis and in the clinical management of hypocalcemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 179
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified within the functional category of hormones and is a member of the broader group of parathyroid hormones and analogues. Calcitonin (spelled kalcitonin) is noted to have a relationship with parathormon, reflecting the physiological interplay between these calcium‑regulating hormones. Teriparatide, a synthetic analogue of parathyroid hormone, is likewise included in the class of parathyroid hormones and analogues. Together, these connections illustrate how parathormon, calcitonin, and teriparatide are organized within hormone taxonomy and underscore their coordinated roles in calcium homeostasis and related therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1903
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified within the functional group “hormones by function.” It is employed therapeutically to treat نقص كالسيوم الدم (hypocalcemia, a deficiency of calcium in the blood). Parathormon has a physiological relationship with kalcitonin (calcitonin); the two hormones interact to regulate calcium homeostasis, often exerting opposing effects to maintain appropriate serum calcium levels. Understanding the role of parathormon in calcium deficiency treatment and its interplay with kalcitonin is essential for effective management of disorders of calcium metabolism.

retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon is a hormone and calcium‑regulating substance that belongs to the group of parathyroid hormones and analogues. It functions within the broader category of hormones and agents that control calcium metabolism and is directly related to the hormone calcitonin, highlighting the interplay between these two regulators of calcium homeostasis.
